brief_document.title: Timer recovery: startup sweep and periodic recovery tick
brief_document.purpose: Build the recovery tier of the two-tier timer. A timer whose owning process slept across a VM restart was never armed on the live wheel; recovery polls the event store for elapsed timers and fires them. This is what makes a month-long sleep durable. It reuses AT-002's fire-once-guarded fire path so the live and recovery tiers never double-fire.
brief_document.task: Implement a recovery component that, on engine startup and on a periodic tick, polls EventStore::expired_timers(now) and fires each due timer that has not already been recorded as fired or cancelled, by delegating to the AT-002 fire path. The recovery-tick interval is supplied by engine configuration, never hardcoded. Recovery must be idempotent.

requirements.title: Startup recovery sweep
requirements.spec: WHEN the engine starts, THE SYSTEM SHALL poll EventStore::expired_timers(now) once and, for each entry whose timer is not already terminal, fire it via the AT-002 fire path (record TimerFired + deliver).
acceptance: recovery.rs defines a recover_on_startup(now) routine that calls expired_timers(now), filters out timers already recorded as TimerFired or TimerCancelled, and fires the remainder through the TimerService fire path.
Firing reuses AT-002's fire-once-guarded path so a startup sweep and any live wheel entry cannot double-fire.
A test schedules a timer with a past fire_at against InMemoryStore (simulating a timer elapsed while down), runs the startup sweep, and asserts TimerFired is recorded and delivered.
A test with a timer whose fire_at is in the future asserts the startup sweep does not fire it.

requirements.title: Periodic recovery tick
requirements.spec: WHILE the engine is running, THE SYSTEM SHALL periodically re-poll expired_timers at an engine-configured interval and fire any newly-due, non-terminal timers, as a safety net for timers whose owning process was not resident on the wheel.
acceptance: A recovery loop runs on an interval supplied by engine configuration (a Duration parameter / config field), NOT a hardcoded constant (CO10).
Each tick polls expired_timers(now) and fires due, non-terminal timers via the same fire path as R1.
The interval value is injected (constructor/config), and a test drives the tick logic directly with an injected clock rather than waiting real wall-clock time (CO9).
A test advances the injected clock past a timer's fire_at, triggers a tick, and asserts the timer fires exactly once.

requirements.title: Idempotent recovery
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL make recovery idempotent. WHEN recovery runs again over a timer already recorded as fired or cancelled, THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT fire it again and SHALL NOT deliver a duplicate message.
acceptance: Running the startup sweep twice in a row over the same store fires each due timer exactly once total (the second run is a no-op for already-fired timers).
A timer recorded as TimerCancelled is never fired by recovery.
A test runs recovery twice and asserts exactly one TimerFired per due timer across both runs.
A test cancels a timer (TimerCancelled present), then runs recovery, and asserts no TimerFired is recorded.

boundaries: SHALL NOT reimplement the fire path — it reuses AT-002's fire-once-guarded TimerService fire.
SHALL NOT hardcode the recovery-tick interval — it is engine-configured (CO10).

SHALL NOT spawn or own the OS/runtime task that drives the periodic tick at production cadence — the brief provides the tick logic and an injected interval; wiring the live driver into the engine runtime is AE's startup responsibility. (Note this split.)
SHALL NOT use wall-clock sleeps in tests — inject the clock (CO9).
verification: cargo test -p aion passes, including idempotency tests.
No hardcoded interval constant (CO10); the interval is injected.
No #[allow], #[expect], or #[ignore] directives in new code.

checklist.text: On engine startup the timer service polls EventStore::expired_timers(now) and, for each entry whose TimerFired has not been recorded, records TimerFired and delivers it to the owning workflow process.
A periodic recovery tick (interval engine-configured, not hardcoded) re-polls expired_timers as a safety net for timers whose owning process was not resident on the wheel.
Recovery is idempotent: re-running it does not double-fire a timer already recorded as fired or cancelled.

stories.text: As the engine, I want a timer fired exactly once whether it fires on the live wheel or via recovery so that replay sees one TimerFired, never two and never none.
As the engine, I want to recover elapsed timers on startup and on a periodic tick so that a workflow that slept across a restart wakes correctly.

constraints.text: unsafe_code = deny across the cluster's modules.
lib.rs/mod.rs are declarations and re-exports only.
Library errors use thiserror domain types; no anyhow in library code; no unwrap/expect in library code; lock poisoning handled explicitly.

These modules live in the aion crate. The seam with AE (workflow process lifecycle, supervision, module loading, residency resolution) is via an explicit engine-facing handle/trait this cluster consumes; this cluster does not manage process lifecycle.

Every interaction that changes what a workflow observed is recorded as an aion-core Event before/at the point of delivery; queries record nothing. No code path delivers a recordable observation without recording it (no silent observation).

Durable timers go through EventStore::schedule_timer / expired_timers; all AT event appends (TimerStarted/Fired/Cancelled, SignalReceived, ChildWorkflow*) go through the target workflow's single Recorder via the engine seam (AD CO6) — never a direct EventStore::append and no bespoke side-channel persistence outside the aion-store contract.

Tests run against aion-store's InMemoryStore; timer expiry, signal delivery + durability, query reply, child spawn, and all/race/map each have explicit test coverage. Time-dependent tests inject the clock — no wall-clock sleeps in tests.
Timer poll intervals, query timeouts, and recovery-tick cadence are engine-configured, never hardcoded defaults (per the no-assumed-defaults rule).

resolved_context.intention: Define the engine-side mechanisms that let a running workflow process interact with the outside world and coordinate concurrent work: durable timers, signal delivery, read-only queries, child-workflow spawning, and the all/race/map concurrency primitives. A workflow process sleeps for a month and wakes; an external system signals an awaiting workflow and the workflow continues in microseconds; an operator queries a live workflow without perturbing it; a parent spawns children and fans work out across the BEAM. These are the interaction surfaces, and they must be both microsecond-fast on the live path and exactly recoverable after a crash. Every interaction that changes what a workflow observed (a timer firing, a signal arriving, a child completing) is recorded as an aion-core Event so that AD's replay returns the same observation without re-waiting; every interaction that observes without changing (a query) is never recorded. The work here is the live half of durable execution — recording the observable, delivering it over beamr's native mailbox and timer wheel, and recovering it on restart — paired with AD's replay half that reads those same events back.

provenance.quote: Define the engine-side mechanisms that let a running workflow process interact with the outside world and coordinate concurrent work: durable timers, signal delivery, read-only queries, child-workflow
